title: practical english usage - Part 127
content:
For expressions like every two years, every three steps, ►     130.9.

She had a child holding on to each hand, (     . . . every hand.) not The business makes less money each/every year, (     .. .-each/every years:) three or more.

talk about two or more people or things; every is normally used to talk about Each and every are both normally used with singular nouns. Each can be used to 1  each      with    two    or  more;    every      with    three     or  more 154  each      and   e very:    the   difference For the difference between each and every, ►     154.

The plans each have certain advantages and disadvantages.

You are each right in a different way.

We can each apply for our own membership card.

They have each been told.

nouns, pronouns and verbs are used.

all and some adverbs (for details of word order, ►    200). In this case plural When  each refers to the subject, it can also go with a verb in mid-position, like 5  with    the   verb I've got five brothers, and each (one/of them) lives in a different city.

each of them is more common in an informal style.

We   can drop a noun after each, if the meaning is clear. However, each one or 4 without      a  noun They cost £3.50  each. I bought the girls two pairs of trainers each.

amounts and quantities.

However, each can come  at the end of a clause in expressions referring to exact 1 sent the secretaries each a Christmas card.

/ want them each to make their own decision.

She kissed them each on the forehead, (          She ktssed-them-eaeh/) end of a clause.

Each can follow an object (direct or indirect), but does not normally come at the 3  position      with    object Each of them has problems.

A  following verb is normally singular.

The children have both gone to bed. My sisters both work in education. -> We can both swim.          Those oranges were both bad.

in 'mid-position', like all and each (for details of word order, ►    200).

When  both refers to the subject of a clause, it can go with the verb, 5 position      with    verb not 'Who did she invite?' 'Both of us.' (     Hs both.) (sn i ... them both;) ‘ Who broke the window - Sarah or Alice?' 'It was both of them'.

But this structure is not used in complements (after be) or in short answers.

She's invited us both. Emily sends you both her love.

We can put both after pronouns used as objects.

Emily sends both of you her love.

Both of them can come tomorrow. She's invited both of us.

can be a subject or object.

With personal pronouns, we  use both of + us/you/them. Both of us/you/them 4 personal       pronouns:      both      of not both (the) children (     the both children) Note that we  do not put the before both, 3   the    not   used    before     both He lost both parents when he was a child.

She's eaten both chops, (     ... both of chops) We   often drop the or a possessive after both; of is not used in this case.

He lost both (of) his parents when he was a child.

She's eaten both (of) the chops. Both (of) these oranges are bad.

equally possible.

Before a noun with a determiner (e.g. the, my, these), both and both of are 2 both      and   both      of brothers carried the piano upstairs - they didn’ t each carry it separately.) My two brothers carried the piano upstairs. (More natural than Both my When the meaning is not 'each of two’ , we  normally use a different expression.

Both my parents were born in Scotland.

Both means 'each of two’ .

155  both      __     __ For more details, ►     153 (each) and >151  (every).

Each of them spoke for five minutes, (          Every of them...) not They each said what they thought. (Birr       They every.. .) Each can be used in some structures where every is impossible.

She's lost nearly every friend she had. (     ... nearly each friend...) without exception, which  stress the idea of a whole group.

We   do not use each with words and expressions like almost, practically, nearly or 3 structures Section13 Determiners:       Quantifiers 156 grammar •        either For either... or, ►    228.   For not... either, neither and nor, ►    227-228.
